title: Ergodic and Discrete Time Crystal Phases in Periodically Kicked Many-Body Quantum Systems: An Analytical Study
abstract: We analytically study the time evolution of the expectation values of observables in periodically kicked many-body quantum systems. Starting from an initial state, we compute both the transient and the long-time properties of the observables. Our derivation explains the criteria and the mechanism that lead to the infinite-temperature statistical average of observables at long times, irrespective of the initial state. When the criteria are violated, the observables oscillate with time. These oscillations are subharmonic and robust to small perturbations, suggesting the emergence of a discrete time crystal phase. We demonstrate these features explicitly in periodically kicked nonintegrable spin chains. For a spin chain with two kicks per cycle, we show that the kicked chain can exhibit an ergodic or a discrete-time crystal phase for the same kicking strengths, depending on the initial state preparation. We complement our time-evolution study of observables with the spectral form factor of these kicked models.
summary: This paper provides an analytical study of how periodically driven many-body systems transition between ergodic and discrete time crystal phases. By using a random phase approximation and diagrammatic techniques, the authors explain how the long-time behavior of observables is determined by the eigenvalues of a stochastic matrix. The work demonstrates that the emergence of time crystals is linked to specific symmetries and can even be sensitive to the initial state of the system.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanisms behind thermalization and the emergence of discrete time crystal (DTC) phases in periodically driven, many-body quantum systems.
main_result: The authors analytically derive the criteria for the system to reach an infinite-temperature statistical average (ergodicity) versus exhibiting robust subharmonic oscillations (DTC phase), showing that the phase can depend on initial state preparation in certain models.
method: The study employs a path integral decomposition of expectation values combined with a Random Phase Approximation (RPA) and a diagrammatic expansion to calculate next-to-leading-order corrections.
model_or_system: Periodically kicked, nonintegrable, disordered, strongly interacting many-body quantum systems, specifically focusing on long-range transverse-field Ising spin chains.
key_observables: Local magnetization, total energy, and the spectral form factor (SFF).
important_parameters: Kicking strength (h), disorder strengths (delta epsilon, delta U0), interaction exponent (alpha), and driving period (tau_p).
main_assumptions: The Random Phase Approximation (RPA) assumes phases are independent and uniformly distributed; the study also focuses on the strong disorder limit.
figures_summary: Figures show the time evolution of magnetization and energy, comparisons between numerical results and RPA (including NLO corrections), and the behavior of the spectral form factor in different phases.
paper_structure: The paper begins with an analytical framework for time evolution using path integrals, moves to the derivation of thermalization and DTC criteria via matrix analysis, provides numerical validation of the RPA and its corrections, and concludes with a detailed study of spectral statistics and symmetry-driven dynamics.
why_interesting: This paper provides a rigorous analytical link between the algebraic properties of the Floquet operator and the emergence of non-equilibrium phases like DTCs, which is central to understanding many-body dynamics and Floquet engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Averaging over 320 realizations of disorder is performed for direct numerical simulation in each case.
